implementation.process_boundary: Supervised BEAM Port running a local child process over newline-delimited JSON on stdio.
implementation.supervision: ElixirIngary.Application supervises ElixirIngary.StarlarkPolicySidecar. The GenServer owns one Port and restarts it after timeout or child exit.
dependency_choices: Rust sidecar keeps Starlark execution outside BEAM schedulers.
stdio Port avoids a network listener and is deterministic for ExUnit.
Jason is reused for the Elixir side of the JSON protocol.

trace.notes: Trace records policy/rule id, selected action, result and reason. Source spans were not attempted in this timebox.

safety_observations: No loader is installed, so Starlark load/import attempts fail closed.
No filesystem or network APIs are provided in Globals::standard, so open/http attempts fail closed as missing variables.
Runaway policies are bounded at the Elixir call boundary. On timeout the GenServer returns block and restarts the Port, killing the child process.
Errors are sanitized to a first-line, control-character-stripped message capped at 240 characters before crossing back into receipts/tests.
latency_observations: Warm ExUnit endpoint calls were generally sub-millisecond to low-millisecond in local logs.
The first sidecar call after application start paid process startup cost around 14ms when the Rust binary was already built.
If the binary is absent, the GenServer attempts a one-time cargo build before opening the Port; this moves cold build cost to startup instead of policy evaluation timeout.
limitations: Regex helpers are not exposed yet; the covered history scenario uses literal substring matching from the visible fixture.
Only a prototype test endpoint is wired; synthetic chat/simulate routing is not governed by Starlark yet.
The Port worker serializes policy evaluations and is not suitable for production concurrency without pooling or per-request workers.
Timeout enforcement is at the process boundary rather than interpreter fuel/instruction counting.

findings.finding: The first committed GenServer shape could accept a second evaluation while one Port response was pending and overwrite the waiting caller. The amended version fails closed with sidecar already evaluating a policy instead of corrupting request/response matching.
The single Port worker is adequate for a deterministic spike endpoint but would need a pool, per-request workers, or upstream backpressure before production routing integration.

The sidecar protocol and error normalization are intentionally small and readable. The largest quality debt is that timeout enforcement lives in the BEAM wrapper rather than explicit interpreter fuel.
Tests cover behavior through the endpoint and native evaluator, including fail-closed negatives and busy-call fail-closed behavior. They do not yet prove regex matching.

viability_verdict: Viable for the Elixir backend as an architecture spike. A supervised local sidecar gives clear fail-closed behavior without risking BEAM scheduler starvation. Production viability depends on adding fuel/instruction limits or per-request process isolation, regex/helper APIs, pooled concurrency, and routing integration.
